coredata.dc:title: High-throughput study examining the wide benefit of Li substitution in oxide cathodes for Na-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Shipeng
Abdolhosseini, Marzieh
Saglio, Leyth
Li, Yixuan
Kamel, Marc
Lavertu, Jean-Danick
Bazylevych, Stephanie
Saïbi, Valentin
Cabelguen, Pierre-Etienne
Kumakura, Shinichi
McCalla, Eric
coredata.dc:description: The stability of Na-ion cathodes is crucial for the widespread adoption of these sustainable battery technologies. Air stability, a critical factor, impacts the synthesis, storage, electrochemical performance, and safety of cathode materials from laboratory research through to commercial manufacturing. Poor air stability of these cathodes leads to disastrous electrochemical performance. Despite significant advancement in understanding air stability of these materials, a comprehensive strategy to universally enhance the air stability of layered oxides remains undeveloped. Here, we use high-throughput methods to systematically study the impact of substituting Li into 24 different sodium layered oxides. The compositions include all of the currently studied structures such as P2, P3 and O3. From a structural point of view, this substitution is quite facile and generally Li integrates smoothly into the structures. Remarkably, lithium strongly enhances air stability across all 24 compositions as determined from the large set of 96 XRD patterns. The improvement in air stability is thus established as a universal benefit of lithium substitution. While a number of Li-doped samples show lower capacities than in their undoped counterparts, two Li-doped samples demonstrate both improved electrochemical performance and complete air stability under harsh aging conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Na-ion battery cathodes
High-throughput experimental testing
Air stability
Battery performance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by NSERC under the auspices of a discovery grant and the Canadian Foundation for Innovation. MA acknowledges a graduate student scholarship from the FRQNT. During the preparation of the draft of this manuscript the authors used Chatgpt in order to polish the writing. After using this tool/service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic workflow of the high-throughput methodology incorporating an air/moisture-accelerated aging protocol. (b) Sodium dissolution triggered by air and moisture, leading to the formation of NaOH and Na2CO3 on the particle surface. (c) Structural transformation of the layered materials due to water insertion.
XRD patterns of (a) undoped and (b) Li-doped materials. Light spectra show the XRD patterns after keeping materials at RH > 96% for 26 days.

Extracted parameters from X-ray diffraction (a) the fraction of total pristine phases that remains after accelerated aging (b) crystallite size L and, electrochemistry (c) discharge capacity of the first cycle (d) capacity retention after 10 cycles.
The voltage curves of the first 1.5 cycles of samples in the combinatorial cell. Undoped samples and Li-doped samples are shaded in blue and yellow respectively.
Discharge capacity per cycle graphs for all 48 samples. Dark blue and orange dots show the capacity of undoped and Li-doped materials respectively.

SEM images of fresh (a) and aged (b) sample 4a (Na0.66Fe0.5Mn0.5O2), fresh (c) and aged (d) sample 4b (Na0.66Li0.1Fe0.4Mn0.5O2), fresh (e) and aged (f) sample 10a (Na0.66Fe0.4Ni0.1Mn0.5O2), and fresh (g) and aged (h) sample 10b (Na0.66Li0.1Fe0.3Ni0.1Mn0.5O2).
List of samples and phase identification both before and after accelerated aging.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * These phases all showed significant changes in the XRD pattern after aging, either a shift in peak position, or a broadening of peaks such that in these cases a high phase retention does not ensure air stability in these materials.
** This scan has unidentified peaks as shown in Fig. S1.

head.ce:title: High-throughput study examining the wide benefit of Li substitution in oxide cathodes for Na-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, McGill University, Montreal, QC H3A 0B8, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, McGill University, Montreal, QC H3A 0B8, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Umicore, 31 rue du marais, Brussels BE-1000, Belgium
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bUmicore, 31 rue du marais, Brussels BE-1000, Belgium

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The stability of Na-ion cathodes is crucial for the widespread adoption of these sustainable battery technologies. Air stability, a critical factor, impacts the synthesis, storage, electrochemical performance, and safety of cathode materials from laboratory research through to commercial manufacturing. Poor air stability of these cathodes leads to disastrous electrochemical performance. Despite significant advancement in understanding air stability of these materials, a comprehensive strategy to universally enhance the air stability of layered oxides remains undeveloped. Here, we use high-throughput methods to systematically study the impact of substituting Li into 24 different sodium layered oxides. The compositions include all of the currently studied structures such as P2, P3 and O3. From a structural point of view, this substitution is quite facile and generally Li integrates smoothly into the structures. Remarkably, lithium strongly enhances air stability across all 24 compositions as determined from the large set of 96 XRD patterns. The improvement in air stability is thus established as a universal benefit of lithium substitution. While a number of Li-doped samples show lower capacities than in their undoped counterparts, two Li-doped samples demonstrate both improved electrochemical performance and complete air stability under harsh aging conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request. The raw XRD data is also available on the Zenodo repository using DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.14947576.

ce:section.ce:para: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have recently gained significant attention as promising short-term alternatives to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Moreover, SIBs can offer cost advantages, particularly for large-scale energy storage applications where the lower cost of sodium compared to lithium becomes a significant factor [1–3]. Among all components of SIBs, cathode materials play a pivotal role, as they typically constrain key metrics related to battery performance. Within the diverse types of cathode materials proposed for SIBs up to now, layered transition metal oxides, NaxMO2 (M represents one or a mixture of transition metals), stand out as some of the most attractive [4,5].Three types of structures are commonly reported for P2, P3, and O3 materials, where "P" and "O" refer to the sodium chemical environments in either prismatic or octahedral structures, respectively, while the numbers denote the repeating units in the layer stacking structure. These layered oxides demonstrate promising energy densities, excellent cyclability, and can utilize facile synthesis methods [6,7].

Despite their excellent electrochemical performance, layered transition metal oxides in SIBs face significant challenges related to air instability and long-term storage [8,9]. All cases of air instability to date lead to dreadful electrochemical performance such that important additional costs (over the established Li-ion infrastructures) must be incurred that compromise the advantages of SIBs [10,11]. Various mechanisms have been proposed to elucidate the aging processes involving air and moisture in different compositions and types of materials. These materials interact with atmospheric components such as H2O, CO2 and O2, leading to irreversible chemical and physical degradation, including phase transitions, water insertion, and the formation of sodium carbonate [12,13]. Due to their expanded interslab spacing, NaxMO2 are more susceptible to reactions with CO2 and H2O in ambient air compared to lithium analogs. Different crystal structures of NaxMO2 with different crystal structures (O3, P2, and P3) and varying compositions undergo air-induced degradation through distinct pathways. For O3 materials, sodium ions can leach from the layered structure during storage in air as shown in Fig. 1 b. Moreover, Na+ may undergo spontaneous exchange with H+ under high humidity, forming NaOH, which subsequently reacts with CO2 to generate Na2CO3 and NaHCO3. This process often leads to phase transitions from the O3 to Na-deficient P3 structures, identifiable by changes in X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns [14]. Additionally, water molecules can insert into sodium layers, exacerbating structural deterioration, which is particularly problematic in P2 materials where the large prismatic Na spacing facilitates the insertion of H2O and CO2 into the layered structure, expanding the interslab distance and accelerating Na dissolution and structural transitions. XRD analysis reveals distinctive hydrated phases with expanded Na layer spacings, exemplified by the appearance of new layered peaks (00l) at lower scattering angles [15].Thus, for all degradation mechanisms, XRD proves effective in quantifying the damage when sufficiently aggressive test conditions are used [5].

Previously, we used our high-throughput methods to study air/moisture stability too. In refs․ [16,17], we showed that the air stability of Na-Fe-Mn-O materials is highly correlated to their compositions and phases. A number of dopants improved the air stability of the cathodes, with Li ranking as best in both studies. Building on previous successful high-throughput methodologies and accelerated air aging tests [16–19], we aim to establish the benefit of Li as a universal dopant across varied compositions of layered oxides with various structures. Herein, we therefore apply fast and reliable high-throughput methods developed in refs․ [20–22] to study the impact of lithium in 24 different compositions/structures as illustrated in Fig. 1. The materials were selected based on past literature and include P2, O3, and P3 phases as well as mixtures thereof. To the authors' knowledge, this represents the first demonstration of Li doping universally improving air stability in sodium layered oxides.

All samples were prepared by applying high-throughput sol-gel synthesis as described in detail in [23]. In summary, 2M solutions of NaNO3, Fe(NO3)3, Mn(NO3)2 and Ni(NO3)2 and 1M solutions of Ti(C4H9O)4, Co(NO3), LiNO3 and Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O (all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) were prepared and dispensed into an 8 × 8 well plate according to the stoichiometry of each sample. A total volume of 40 μL was dispensed into each well. Table 1 shows the chemical composition of all samples. Then, citric acid (3M) as the cleating agent was added to the above precursor solutions at a ratio of 1:1 to the total metal cations. Samples were heated at 65 °C for 2 days until homogeneous precursor gels were formed. Next, the resulting gels were crushed into powder and transferred onto an alumina plate. An aluminum smokestack with 64 airflow vents was mounted on the powders to separate samples by providing independent combustion chambers to prevent cross-contamination. Then, samples were heated at 400 °C for 2 h at a rate of 2 °C min−1 to combust nitrates and citric acid. After the smokestack was removed, samples were calcinated at 850 °C for 12 h at a rate of 5 °C min−1 in a box furnace in the ambient air and then they cooled to the room temperature at the rate of 5 °C min−1. In total, 24 different undoped samples and 24 Li-doped (10 %) samples were made for this study.

Following the above synthesis, samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) in high-throughput mode (8h for 48 samples). A Panalytical Empyrean diffractometer with a Mo source (60 kV, 40 mA) and GalaPIX area detector were used to collect the XRD patterns in transmission mode. All the scans were done in the 4–30° scattering angle range of Mo Kα radiation (λ = 0.70926 Å for Kα1), which is approximately equal to the 10–70° angle range for Cu radiation. After completing all scans, the Mo Kα2 peaks were removed, and the angles were converted to the angles corresponding to Cu-Kα1 (λ = 1.54051 Å) to make comparison with the literature easier. This conversion is fully described in [24]. Overall, 96 XRD measurements were done for this study including 48 spectra for pristine samples and 48 spectra for aged samples. The XRD patterns were identified and refined using Panalytical's HighScore Plus software. Multiple-phase Rietveld refinement was then performed on all XRD patterns, and the results are summarized in Table 1.

To accelerate this study, all the electrochemical tests were conducted using combinatorial cells enabling the simultaneous cycling of 64 cathode materials. The design and functionality of these cells are elaborated in detail in refs․ [20,22,24]. Briefly, sixty-four parallel channels are incorporated within a printed circuit board (PCB, Optima Tech). Each channel features a nickel pad coated in aluminum foil onto which cathode materials are drop-casted. All the cathode electrodes in this study contained 2 mg of active material (giving an active material loading of about 20 mg/cm2) and mixed with 20 wt. % carbon black. They were placed on aluminum contact pads and then a mixture of N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) with polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was drop cast onto the samples. The final electrodes contained roughly 20 wt. % PVDF. Subsequently, PCB was kept at 50 °C overnight to allow for the vaporization of NMP and achieve a loading of 20 mg cm–2 of active material. Then, the assembly of the combinatorial cells were done in an argon-filled glovebox. 1M sodium perchlorate in propylene carbonate (PC) with 2 wt. % fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) was used as the electrolyte of combinatorial cells. Sodium metal foil and GF/D glass microfibre prefilter were used as the anode, and separator respectively. The electrochemistry of the samples in the combinatorial cells were investigated by performing cyclic voltammetry (CV) within the voltage range of 2.0 to 4.3 V vs. Na/Na+ at a scan rate of 0.1 V h−1 on a lab-built high-throughput electrochemical system which is introduced in [25]. All the CVs were converted to the voltage profiles by integrating Idt to demonstrate capacity as a function of voltage. The capacity, average voltage, overpotential and capacity retention (after 10 cycles) were extracted for all the pristine undoped, and Li-doped samples from the processed data.

To evaluate air and moisture stability, an accelerated aging protocol was performed in this study. All the 48 undoped and Li-doped samples were subjected to aggravated humidity conditions to evaluate the long-term effects of exposure to expected levels of H2O and CO2 within a shorter timeframe. Samples were kept in an enclosed system for 1 month at RH 45% (near ambient in our lab), then another month at RH 96% using a saturated K2SO4 solution to maintain the humidity level. The humidity level was monitored on a daily basis with a humidity meter during the whole experiment and was maintained at 95–97% and water was added to the saturated solution as needed to maintain the humid environment. The temperature was maintained at 23 ± 1 °C. Then, XRD was performed, and the patterns were compared with the ones of the fresh samples.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted to study the surface morphology of selected samples and investigate the effect of Li doping. SEM images were taken using FEI Quanta 450 Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific Nexsa G2 XPS with a monochromatic Al Kα source (15 mA, 15 kV). Peak fitting was conducted using the Avantage software. XPS spectra for the Mn, Fe, and Ni K-edges were collected for samples 10a and 10b, both before (pristine) and after charging to 4.3 V. Swagelok cells were assembled in an argon-filled glovebox using Na foil as the anode, a GF/D glass microfiber prefilter as the separator, and the same electrolyte as in the combinatorial cells. The cathodes consisted of 2 mg of active material and approximately 20 wt. % carbon black. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) at a scan rate of 0.1 V h⁻¹ was performed to charge the samples to 4.3 V. After charging, the Swagelok cells were opened inside an argon-filled glovebox to retrieve and rinse the cathode material. Three rinsing cycles with dimethyl carbonate (DMC) were performed, and the remaining powder was then dried overnight under ambient argon gas in the glovebox. Finally, the samples were transferred to the XPS facility using an air-sensitive sample holder.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed using an SP150 Biologic instrument. Samples 10a and 10b were selected for the EIS study. Prior to conductivity measurements, the pristine samples were pelletized and gold-coated. To investigate the effect of aging on resistance, the pellets were placed in a sealed box at 96% humidity immediately after the initial EIS measurements and stored for seven days. A saturated K₂SO₄ solution was used to maintain the humidity level, which was monitored daily using a humidity meter. The temperature was maintained at 23 ± 1 °C throughout the experiment. After one week of accelerated aging, EIS measurements were performed on the same pellets. The thicknesses of the 5 mm diameter pellets were 0.410 mm and 0.396 mm for samples 10a and 10b, respectively. The weights of the samples were 19.9 mg and 19.6 mg for samples 10a and 10b, respectively.

As previously discussed, air stability is crucial for the practical application and commercialization of cathode materials. Accurately identifying and quantifying air stability can be challenging. To address this, we utilized high-throughput XRD and accelerated aging methodology. A set of 48 samples were stored under high humidity conditions simultaneously. Fig. 2 , S1-S2 show the XRD patterns of undoped and Li-doped samples both before and after accelerated aging. The patterns were visually compared to reference patterns to identify existing phases, and Rietveld refinement was used to fit the data. This allowed us to quantify the phase concentrations, which were then used to determine the air stability of each sample, as summarized in Table 1. Primary phase retentions were calculated for all samples after one month at RH 45% followed by one month at RH > 96%. These results are illustrated in Fig. 3 a. High humidity conditions were chosen to simulate extreme weather conditions and maritime shipping environments.

To facilitate comparison, we establish benchmark samples studied previously for their air stability. P2-Na0.66MnO2 is recognized for its air sensitivity and tendency to incorporate water molecules within its sodium layers. Even at low humidity (RH 18%), structural changes detectable by XRD can occur within three days. Our prior studies revealed that Na0.66Mn0.5Fe0.5O2 (sample 4a), with iron substitution, maintains its P2 structure for six months under controlled low humidity conditions—a useful benchmark. Exposure to high humidity (RH 96%) for one month resulted in only 20% retention of the P2 phase, with significant transformation to the hydration phase. Based on this accelerated aging data, materials that retain over 20% of their initial phase under such extreme conditions can be considered stable for at least six months under low humidity (RH < 10%). It is important to note that this high humidity protocol represents an exceptionally harsh condition for assessing air stability; in contrast, Li-doped samples exhibited minimal phase transition at 45% RH over one month of storage. Therefore, following this one-month period at 45% RH, we conducted the high humidity test (RH 96%). Sample 4b (same composition as 4a except that 10% Li is added), by contrast, retains 99.4% of the initial phases with only 0.6% conversion to birnessite. This dramatically demonstrates the benefit of Li doping, at least in the least stable materials. In fact, as shown in Figs. 2, 3a and Table 1, Li-doping systematically improved the air stability: not only all the Li-doped samples are more stable than undoped materials but the Li-doped versions of samples 4, 5, 9, 10, 13, 15, 16, 17, 19, 23 and 24 all showed approximately 100% phase retention. The a and c lattice parameters both for pristine and aged samples are also reported in Table S1. The samples that showed high phase retention show minor changes in the lattice parameters before and after accelerated aging, the most important change being a small increase in the c lattice parameters. These values are plotted as sodium spacing (distance between neighboring Na layers) in Fig S6 and we note that samples 10b, 11b and 24b all show no change in the c lattice parameter after aging. It is noteworthy that the phase retention discussed here pertains specifically to phase identification. Some samples may still experience minor alterations, as indicated by slight peak broadening or shifting. This list of materials includes the well-known phases for SIBs cathode materials including P2, O3 and P3 phases from which it might be concluded that Li substitution can universally and dramatically improve the air/moisture stability of sodium layered transition metal oxides. It is also worth mentioning that for some cases (e.g. samples 23 Na0.66Ni0.35Ti0.65O2) the phase retention was increased from 0% to 100% with the addition of lithium.

In order to explore to what extent the improvements in the air stability seen in the XRD extend to the surface, we performed EIS measurements on the 10a and 10b samples both before and after aging. These samples were selected for further study as they represent one of the cases where both air stability and specific capacity improves with Li doping as discussed further below. Fig. S3 shows the results. While both pristine samples show semicircles of comparable resistance (near 20 kΩ), there is a stark difference in the aged samples after 7 days of aging. The 10b sample increased its resistance to about 75 kΩ, while the 10a sample shows a resistance of about 500 kΩ. Thus, the impedance of the undoped sample increased by nearly an order of magnitude more than the Li-doped counterpart. This indicates that the stabilization shown in the bulk structure based on XRD are indeed meaningful even in measurements where the surface plays an important role. The fact that the 10b sample did show an increase in resistance also indicates that further work is needed to provide extra stability at the surface of particles, while the current study certainly shows that optimal composition is necessary to stabilize the bulk of samples.

To further investigate the impact of Li doping on the electrochemical performance of layered oxide materials, high-throughput cyclic voltammetry (CV) was also performed on all compositions between 2.0 V and 4.3 V. CVs for all 48 samples are plotted in Fig. S4 and corresponding voltage curves are plotted in Fig. 4 . The performance during extended cycling is shown in Fig. 5 . The 24 sets of materials show widely varying CVs and electrochemical performance, consistent with past literature on related materials. We observe a range of materials, with some exhibiting very large overpotentials and others displaying relatively small overpotentials. Specific capacity, overpotential, average voltage and negative capacities were all extracted for each sample and summarized in Table S2 and Fig. 3b,d. The varied electrochemical results are, as intended, a direct result of the breadth of structures/compositions selected. The results are also consistent with those reported previously. As we demonstrate quantitatively in our previous work[23], the combinatorial electrochemistry does indeed yield battery metrics consistent with that published elsewhere. It is noteworthy that Li doping does not contribute to capacity, as it rather substitutes for redox-active transition metal (TM). The 10% doping level was chosen to also avoid anionic redox processes. Fig. 3b,d shows that there is no systematic benefit to adding Li in terms of battery performance. Most materials are comparable to the undoped in terms of specific capacity with a few notable exceptions. Samples 4, 10 and 24 all show improved discharge capacities when doped with Li and these are maintained over 10 cycles (Fig. 5). Samples 10b and 24b also showed outstanding air stability, as discussed previously, making them of particular interest. To explore possible mechanisms giving the improved capacities in these particular samples, we explored the 10a and 10b samples more carefully using XPS to examine both the pristine and fully charged materials. Fig. S8 shows the fitted XPS patterns with relevant extracted parameters in Tables S3 and S4. The results for Ni show a very comparable Ni state in the fully charged materials (average oxidation state of approximately 2.5). Given that 10a and 10b have the same nickel content, this accounts for the same amount of capacity. For iron and manganese, it is the peak splitting (arrows shown in Fig. S8) that correlates to oxidation state as we have reported previously [18], with a smaller splitting indicating a larger oxidation state. Table S4 therefore shows that both Fe and Mn are slightly oxidized in 10b while only Fe is oxidized in the 10a sample; but the differences are relatively small. Given that XPS is a surface technique, we acknowledge that the bulk might show slight variations from this. Nonetheless, based on these results there is little to distinguish 10a and 10b in terms of redox of the transition metals, with the exception that the extra Fe redox in the 10b samples might have contributed to some of the extra capacity seen here with Li doping. We speculate that the rest of the extra capacity when Li is added in 10b comes from anionic redox. However, this is a notoriously difficult mechanism to understand and predict, and our results clearly show that this is not systematic across all Li-doped samples (i.e. only 3 of the 24 showed improved capacity). A more complete understanding of anionic redox is the subject of future works within our group and the current study certainly adds interesting materials to the list of compositions worthy of further study.

The extended cycling in Fig. 5 also shows that sample 9 shows improvement in capacity when Li-doped with a high final capacity (after 10 cycles) of over 155 mAh/g as is the case for 4b and 10b (near 150 mAh/g). The 3 highest capacity materials are: Na0.66Li0.1Fe0.4Mn0.5O2 (4b, mixture of P2+P3), Na0.66Li0.1Fe0.3Mn0.5Ti0.1O2 (9b, P2) and Na0.66Li0.1Fe0.3Ni0.1Mn0.5O2 (10b, P2+P3). In all 3 cases, the subvalent Li substitution may be beneficial in oxidizing Mn to the more stable 4+ state in the pristine materials with suppression of Jahn-Teller effect of Mn3+. By contrast, the low Mn content in sample 7, NaFe0.7Mn0.3O2, shows very little change in performance when Li is added. Similarly, Li addition has a relatively minor impact on the extended cycling (retention after 10 cycles shown in Fig. 3d). There are also a number of cases where the electrochemical performance (both specific capacity and capacity retention) are higher in the undoped materials. Thus, although Li appears to be consistently beneficial for air stability, its impact on electrochemical performance is more variable and we speculate that much of this may be due to the subvalent substitution reducing the TM redox available during the first charge. The series of battery tests ultimately demonstrates that the addition of Li has a relatively minor impact on overall battery metrics, although it does offer some benefits in certain specific compositions. There are several potential mechanisms through which Li doping could negatively affect performance, such as reducing the population of redox-active metals which could decrease capacity, or interfering with layered phase formation and causing Li to remain on the surface, thereby increasing resistance. However, none of these adverse effects were observed. The absence of negative impacts on electrochemical performance is therefore considered favorable.

Prior to a discussion as to the roles that Li might be playing in stabilizing the materials in air, it is important to first establish to what extent lithium is changing the morphology of the materials. Certainly, larger particles are expected to be more stable in air simply due to the reduced surface area. We therefore first evaluate the effect of Li substitution on the crystallite size obtained using the Scherrer equation after correcting for machine broadening. These values are shown in Fig. 3b. There are certainly cases where the Scherrer length increases slightly as Li is added, but it is hardly the universal trend seen in Fig. 3a for the stability in air. For example, samples 7a and 7b show a slightly larger crystallite size in the undoped sample and yet a far improved stability in air in the doped sample. We therefore attribute the far more dramatic improvement in air stability in the doped samples for all others to both crystallite size but predominantly to the benefit of having lithium in the structure as will be discussed in the next section. We also used the Dynamic Light Scattering technique to evaluate the size of samples. Fig. S5 shows the size distribution diagrams for samples 4b and 11b. The mean diameter calculated by intensity are 141.91 nm and 200.03 nm for sample 4b and 11b respectively, in qualitative agreement with our crystallite size analysis (i.e. sizes for 11b are larger).

Given the propensity of these materials to agglomerate into large secondary particles, we further explore the effect of Li substitution on the morphology of the particles using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for two sets of samples (samples 4 and 10). Fig. 6 shows the SEM images for both aged and fresh samples obtained from both undoped and Li-doped materials. The results show that the morphology does not change significantly with Li doping (4a and 4b show similar primary particles and well joined secondary particles, as do 10a and 10b). By contrast, there is significant variation after aging. It is clearly seen that the particles swell in both undoped samples (4a and 10a) forming a glass-like surface (higher exposure may result from the insulation layer of sodium carbonate). The SEM image in Fig. 6b further reveals cracks resulting from water insertion and subsequent structural expansion. However, the Li-doped samples show no such transformation, the morphology looks comparable to that of the fresh materials. Thus, the stability seen in the XRD for the Li-doped samples extends to the particle surfaces on the long-range as viewed by SEM.

Previous literature reported various phenomena and mechanisms to elucidate air/water-introduced structural degradation. For example, in nickel-rich materials, the dissolution of nickel ions also accelerates structural degradation, resulting in the accumulation of NiO on the surface of the particle [26]. Mu et al. conducted a comprehensive study on the water stability of various O3-type materials and found that nearly all O3 structures undergo structural degradation, demonstrating high water sensitivity [27]. Zheng et al. systematically studied the aging process of NaNi0.5Mn0.5O2, observing significant peak broadening in XRD patterns after only 5 days of storage in wet argon [28]. Moreover, total structural collapse was noted after 5 days in wet CO2, highlighting the critical roles of humidity and CO2 in the air. For O3-Na0.7Ni0.35Sn0.65O2, Zhang et al. found that phase transitions could initiate after just 10 min of air exposure, underscoring the extreme air sensitivity of O3 materials [29].

Moreover, following water insertion, the Na-layer slab distance can increase from 5.6 Å to 7.1 Å to accommodate the additional water molecules and the weaker Na-O interactions [16]. For example, hydrated manganese-based materials frequently exhibit a birnessite phase (Na0.29MnO2 0.75H2O) [30], which is also a naturally found manganese ore. Zuo et al․ [31] investigated the extreme moisture sensitivity of P2-Na0.67MnO2. Even under low humidity conditions (RH 18%) for a 3-day storage period, hydration peaks were evident in XRD patterns. At humidity levels exceeding RH 90%, the P2 structure completely transformed into the birnessite phase. The instability of Na0.67Mn0.5Fe0.5O2 was initially investigated by Nazar et al․ [15], who proposed that CO3 2− ions could occupy tetrahedral positions within the layered structure. Furthermore, aged samples exhibited inconsistent charge profiles between the first and second cycles, accompanied by increased overpotential. Although P2 materials are often reported to have better air stability compared to O3 materials, their moisture sensitivity still poses a significant challenge for long-term storage and practical applications [32]. Bachu et al. proposed that tunning the interslab spacing via TM-O bond covalency can manipulate the air/water stability [33]. However, in practice, predicting the effectiveness of this strategy is challenging, as the interslab spacing cannot be accurately assessed until the materials are synthesized and experimentally compared.

The current study shows that Li doping consistently demonstrates excellent effectiveness in improving air stability by mitigating various reactions with air and moisture. Specifically, it is clear here that Li doping can suppress water insertion, phase transitions (such as O3 to P3 and P2 to birnessite phases), and Ni dissolution in Ni-rich materials. In this study, we also compared Li doping with Cu and Ti doping (samples 20–22), which are reported to enhance air stability to some extent. Nevertheless, our findings indicate that Li doping clearly outperforms Cu and Ti doping in its effectiveness. Therefore, we propose that Li doping can serve as a universal strategy to enhance air stability across all compositions and structures of layered oxide materials, owing to its robust ability to suppress diverse air and moisture aging reactions.

In exploring the efficacy of Li doping, we now delve into possible mechanisms underlying its ability to enhance air stability. Several researchers [34–36] have reported that Li doping can occupy sites originally filled by both TM and Na+. Here, given the large number of samples and their relative compositional complexity, it is not trivial to identify the location of Li in all 24 doped materials. Instead, we focus on two materials: 3b which is a P2 material and 7b which is an O3 material. Fig. S7 shows the result of performing Rietveld fits on these two materials and relaxing the site occupations (this is not done in the automated fitting performed for all 96 XRD patterns). In the P2 sample (3b), Li occupancy converges to all Li occupying the Na layer, while in the O3 material it is the opposite: Li occupies the TM layer only. It should be stressed that identifying the location of Li with XRD is not trivial, but it can be done. This result is also logical as there are considerable Na vacancies in the P2 material making it easier for Li to occupy these sites. These two Rietveld fits are certainly consistent with past literature that claim that Li can occupy both sites. Upon substituting for transition metals, Li forms weaker bonding with oxygen (O2−) compared to TM-O bonds. Consequently, within LiO6 octahedra, the electron density of O2− increases, fortifying the interaction between O2− and Na+ ions. This fortification mitigates Na+ dissolution during air storage, thereby stabilizing Na+ sites and suppressing Na+/H+ exchange processes. Additionally, the monovalent nature of Li facilitates the oxidation of transition metals, such as Mn3+ to Mn4+, thereby reducing the Jahn-Teller effect. An overall higher oxidation state for the transition metals is generally viewed as beneficial for air stability as established by Zuo et al․ [31] and Nazar et al․ [37]. Assisted with X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and Fourier-transformed extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra, we have previously confirmed that some Li-doped samples maintained structural integrity akin to pristine samples, without distortion in TM-O local structures [19]. Moreover, heightened levels of transition metals resist oxidation during air exposure, bolstering resistance to oxygen. Density functional theory (DFT) [35] analyses revealed that Li dopants exhibit higher surface adsorption energy ( ΔEa ds
= −0.1 eV) towards water molecules compared to other dopants like Co ( ΔEads
= −1.06 eV) and Fe ( ΔEads
= −1.37 eV), further validating the diminished water affinity post Li doping.

Furthermore, Li+ ions within Na+ slabs act akin to pillars, stabilizing interslab distances. Insertion of water molecules increases these distances from 5.6 Å to 7.1 Å (00l), accommodating additional water molecules and further promoting Na+ ion migration to the surface for reaction with CO2. However, strong O1-Li-O2 interactions with neighboring slabs curtail slab expansion and sliding, thereby enhancing air stability, as also corroborated by 7Li solid-state NMR studies, where 17% Li dopants can be found in Na layer [34]. Consequently, we speculate that the distinctive efficacy of Li doping across such diverse compositions/structures emanates from its unique ability to dope both the TM and Na+ sites. It is also worth keeping in mind that although we find that Li improves air stability in all 24 doped samples, it does not provide the same level of protection in all materials, but nature of the undoped material also plays a significant role, as previously highlighted in ref․ [35].

Na-ion batteries are receiving a great deal of attention as potential green alternatives to Li-ion batteries. However, this necessitates their performance being competitive. Two areas that continue to require improvement are the air stability of the cathode materials and the energy density of the materials. Herein, 48 different materials (24 undoped while the other 24 were identical except for 10% Li substitution) were tested both before and after accelerated aging in humid air. The results showed systematic and strong improvements in air stability for all compositions with facile Li doping. In some, the improved air stability with Li-doping could be partially explained by larger crystallites while others in fact had smaller crystallites than the undoped. As a result, we conclude that the presence of lithium in the structures serves as a universal stabilizer in air for Na layered oxide cathodes. The breadth of structures/compositions tested also demonstrates that Li is effective in suppressing all known mechanisms for the decomposition of these materials in humid air. Further electrochemical testing demonstrated that lithium also enhanced the battery performance of some compositions, though this was not a universal benefit as careful tuning of the various oxidation states of the transition metals is needed in order to ensure that sufficient redox processes are available during battery use. The establishment of a universally beneficial dopant for air stability will help simplify the further design of this important class of materials and thereby guide further research & development.

Shipeng Jia: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Marzieh Abdolhosseini: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Leyth Saglio: Investigation, Data curation. Yixuan Li: Data curation. Marc Kamel: Investigation, Data curation. Jean-Danick Lavertu: Data curation. Stephanie Bazylevych: Data curation. Valentin Saïbi: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Pierre-Etienne Cabelguen: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shinichi Kumakura: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Eric McCalla: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was funded by NSERC under the auspices of a discovery grant and the Canadian Foundation for Innovation. MA acknowledges a graduate student scholarship from the FRQNT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of the draft of this manuscript the authors used Chatgpt in order to polish the writing. After using this tool/service, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2025.146077.
